question: Describe the primary function of the Program Counter (PC) and the Instruction Register (IR) during the fetch phase of the instruction cycle.
expectedPoints: The Program Counter (PC) holds the memory address of the next instruction to be fetched and executed.
During the fetch phase, the value from the PC is sent to the memory address register (MAR) to initiate a read operation.
The instruction fetched from memory is then loaded into the Instruction Register (IR) for decoding and execution by the control unit.

question: Explain the concept of a bus in a computer system. Describe the three common types of system buses and their specific roles in data transfer.
expectedPoints: A bus is a communication pathway consisting of a set of wires that connects major computer components (CPU, memory, I/O).
The Data Bus is bidirectional and carries actual data and instructions between components.
The Address Bus is unidirectional (from CPU) and carries memory addresses to specify where data is to be read from or written to.
The Control Bus carries control and timing signals (e.g., read, write, interrupt) to coordinate the activities of the system.

question: Trace the complete sequence of operations, from fetch to execute, for a simple 'Load' instruction (e.g., LOAD R2, [1000]). Detail the role of the MAR, MDR, PC, IR, and ALU at each stage, and explain how the data moves through the system.
expectedPoints: Fetch: PC provides address to MAR; memory read; instruction copied to MDR, then to IR; PC is incremented.
Decode: Control unit decodes the opcode in the IR to identify it as a Load operation and determines the source address (1000).
Execute (Address Calculation): The effective address (1000) may be sent to the MAR directly or require calculation via the ALU.
Execute (Data Transfer): MAR sends address 1000 to memory; memory read operation; data from memory location 1000 is copied to the MDR.
Execute (Register Write): The data from the MDR is transferred and loaded into the specified destination register (R2).
